title: Module 1 quiz: What Cloudflare actually is

questions.prompt: You enabled WAF rules but they don't seem to apply. Most likely cause?

choices.text: Your DNS record is gray-cloud (DNS-only). Cloudflare features only apply when proxy mode is on (orange cloud). Without proxying, traffic resolves to your origin IP and skips Cloudflare entirely.

questions.explanation: Proxy mode is the on-switch for Cloudflare features. Orange cloud = features active. Gray cloud = Cloudflare serves DNS only; traffic bypasses the edge.

choices.text: Cloudflare picks the user's nearest PoP from a geolocation database.
Cloudflare announces the same IP from many PoPs; BGP automatically delivers packets to the topologically closest PoP. One IP, many physical locations, no client or app config required.
DNS returns different IPs per region.

questions.explanation: Anycast is a BGP-layer pattern. The internet's routing tables resolve the nearest PoP automatically. Cloudflare doesn't pick; the network does.

questions.prompt: Behind Cloudflare, your rate limiter reads `request.remoteAddress` and treats every request as if from a Cloudflare IP. What's wrong?

choices.text: When proxied, the connection's remote IP is Cloudflare's edge — not the user. Read the real client IP from `cf-connecting-ip` (or `X-Forwarded-For`). Most frameworks have middleware to handle this once configured correctly.

questions.explanation: Cloudflare passes the real client IP in headers. Your app must read those for rate limiting, logging, and analytics.

questions.prompt: What's the canonical production pattern for using Cloudflare?

choices.text: Cloudflare in front of your real app on a cloud provider. Cloudflare handles edge concerns (DDoS, latency, caching, some logic via Workers); origin handles application state, complex compute, primary databases.

questions.explanation: Cloudflare in front of origin is the canonical pattern. Edge concerns at the edge; application concerns at origin. Cloudflare-native (everything on Workers/D1/R2) works for specific greenfield workloads but isn't universal.

questions.prompt: A team needs heavy ML inference per request (large models, GPU required). They're considering Workers AI. Right call?

choices.text: Workers AI handles specific models well, but for heavy or custom large-model inference, use dedicated inference platforms (Replicate, HuggingFace, AWS Bedrock, your own GPU). Keep Cloudflare at the edge for routing, caching, and security. Hybrid is the right shape.

questions.explanation: Workers AI is improving but the model lineup is smaller than dedicated inference platforms. For heavy or custom workloads, use specialized inference services. Cloudflare for edge; specialized for compute.
